name: django-pgtrigger

metadata.name: django-pgtrigger
metadata.summary: Postgres trigger support integrated with Django models.

documentation:
# django-pgtrigger

`django-pgtrigger` helps you write [Postgres triggers](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-createtrigger.html) for your Django models.

## Why should I use triggers?

Triggers can solve a variety of complex problems more reliably, performantly, and succinctly than application code.
For example,

* Protecting operations on rows or columns (`pgtrigger.Protect`).
* Making read-only models or fields (`pgtrigger.ReadOnly`).
* Soft-deleting models (`pgtrigger.SoftDelete`).
* Snapshotting and tracking model changes ([django-pghistory](https://django-pghistory.readthedocs.io/)).
* Enforcing field transitions (`pgtrigger.FSM`).
* Keeping a search vector updated for full-text search (`pgtrigger.UpdateSearchVector`).
* Building official interfaces (e.g. enforcing use of `User.objects.create_user` and not `User.objects.create`).
* Versioning models, mirroring fields, computing unique model hashes, and the list goes on...

All of these examples require no overridden methods, no base models, and no signal handling.

## Quick start

Install `django-pgtrigger` with `pip3 install django-pgtrigger` and add `pgtrigger` to `settings.INSTALLED_APPS`.

`pgtrigger.Trigger` objects are added to `triggers` in model `Meta`. `django-pgtrigger` comes with several trigger classes, such as `pgtrigger.Protect`. In the following, we're protecting the model from being deleted:

```python
import pgtrigger

class ProtectedModel(models.Model):
    """This model cannot be deleted!"""

    class Meta:
        triggers = [
            pgtrigger.Protect(name="protect_deletes", operation=pgtrigger.Delete)
        ]
```

When migrations are created and executed, `ProtectedModel` will raise an exception anytime a deletion is attempted.

Let's extend this example further and only protect deletions on inactive objects. In this example, the trigger conditionally runs when the row being deleted (the `OLD` row in trigger terminology) is still active:

```python
import pgtrigger

class ProtectedModel(models.Model):
    """Active object cannot be deleted!"""
    is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True)

    class Meta:
        triggers = [
            pgtrigger.Protect(
                name="protect_deletes",
                operation=pgtrigger.Delete,
                condition=pgtrigger.Q(old__is_active=True)
            )
        ]
```

`django-pgtrigger` uses `pgtrigger.Q` and `pgtrigger.F` objects to conditionally execute triggers based on the `OLD` and `NEW` rows. Combining these Django idioms with `pgtrigger.Trigger` objects can solve a wide variety of problems without ever writing SQL. Users, however, can still use raw SQL for complex cases.

Triggers are installed like other database objects. Run `python manage.py makemigrations` and `python manage.py migrate` to install triggers.

If triggers are new to you, don't worry. The [pgtrigger docs](https://django-pgtrigger.readthedocs.io/) cover triggers in more detail and provide many examples.

## Compatibility

`django-pgtrigger` is compatible with Python 3.9 - 3.13, Django 4.2 - 5.1, Psycopg 2 - 3, and Postgres 13 - 17.

## Documentation

[View the django-pgtrigger docs here](https://django-pgtrigger.readthedocs.io/) to learn more about:

* Trigger basics and motivation for using triggers.
* How to use the built-in triggers and how to build custom ones.
* Installing triggers on third-party models, many-to-many fields, and other advanced scenarios.
* Writing conditional triggers.
* Ignoring triggers dynamically and deferring trigger execution.
* Multiple database, schema, and partitioning support.
* Frequently asked questions, common issues, and upgrading.
* The commands, settings, and module.

## Installation

Install `django-pgtrigger` with:

    pip3 install django-pgtrigger
After this, add `pgtrigger` to the `INSTALLED_APPS` setting of your Django project.

## Other Material

After you've read the docs, check out [this tutorial](https://wesleykendall.github.io/django-pgtrigger-tutorial/) with interactive examples from a Django meetup talk.

The [DjangoCon 2021 talk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Tte3d4JjxCk) also breaks down triggers and shows several examples.

## Contributing Guide

For information on setting up django-pgtrigger for development and contributing changes, view [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md).

## Creators

- [Wes Kendall](https://github.com/wesleykendall)

## Other Contributors

- @jzmiller1
- @rrauenza
- @ralokt
- @adamchainz

- @kekekekule
- @peterthomassen
- @pfouque

functions.name: pgtrigger.utils.connection
documentation:
Obtains the connection used for a trigger / model pair. The database
for the connection is selected based on the write DB in the database
router config.

functions.name: pgtrigger.utils.pg_maj_version
documentation: Return the major version of Postgres that's running

functions.name: pgtrigger.utils.postgres_databases
documentation:
Return postgres databases from the provided list of databases.

If no databases are provided, return all postgres databases

functions.name: pgtrigger.utils.quote
documentation: Conditionally wraps a label in quotes

functions.name: pgtrigger.utils.render_uninstall
documentation: Renders uninstallation SQL

classes.name: pgtrigger.utils.AttrDict
documentation: A dictionary where keys can be accessed as attributes

functions.name: pgtrigger.registry.set
documentation:
Set a trigger in the registry

Args:
    uri: The trigger URI
    model: The trigger model
    trigger: The trigger object

functions.name: pgtrigger.registry.delete
documentation:
Delete a trigger from the registry.

Args:
    uri: The trigger URI

functions.name: pgtrigger.registry.registered
documentation:
Get registered trigger objects.

Args:
    *uris: URIs of triggers to get. If none are provided,
        all triggers are returned. URIs are in the format of
        `{app_label}.{model_name}:{trigger_name}`.

Returns:
    Matching trigger objects.

functions.name: pgtrigger.registry.register
documentation:
Register the given triggers with wrapped Model class.

Args:
    *triggers: Trigger classes to register.

Example:
    Register by decorating a model:

        @pgtrigger.register(
            pgtrigger.Protect(
                name="append_only",
                operation=(pgtrigger.Update | pgtrigger.Delete)
            )
        )
        class MyModel(models.Model):
            pass

Example:
    Register by calling functionally:

        pgtrigger.register(trigger_object)(MyModel)

classes.name: pgtrigger.registry._Registry

methods.name: pgtrigger.registry._Registry.pg_function_names
documentation: The postgres function names of all registered triggers

methods.name: pgtrigger.registry._Registry.by_db_table
documentation: Return the registry keys by db_table, name
All registered triggers for each model

modules.name: pgtrigger.installation
documentation: The primary functional API for pgtrigger

functions.name: pgtrigger.installation.install
documentation:
Install triggers.

Args:
    *uris: URIs of triggers to install. If none are provided,
        all triggers are installed and orphaned triggers are pruned.
    database: The database. Defaults to the "default" database.

functions.name: pgtrigger.installation.prunable
documentation:
Return triggers that are candidates for pruning

Args:
    database: The database. Defaults to the "default" database.

Returns:
    A list of tuples consisting of the table, trigger ID, enablement, and database

functions.name: pgtrigger.installation.prune
documentation:
Remove any pgtrigger triggers in the database that are not used by models.
I.e. if a model or trigger definition is deleted from a model, ensure
it is removed from the database

Args:
    database: The database. Defaults to the "default" database.

functions.name: pgtrigger.installation.enable
documentation:
Enables registered triggers.

Args:
    *uris: URIs of triggers to enable. If none are provided,
        all triggers are enabled.
    database: The database. Defaults to the "default" database.

functions.name: pgtrigger.installation.uninstall
documentation:
Uninstalls triggers.

Args:
    *uris: URIs of triggers to uninstall. If none are provided,
        all triggers are uninstalled and orphaned triggers are pruned.
    database: The database. Defaults to the "default" database.

functions.name: pgtrigger.installation.disable
documentation:
Disables triggers.

Args:
    *uris: URIs of triggers to disable. If none are provided,
        all triggers are disabled.
    database: The database. Defaults to the "default" database.

modules.name: pgtrigger.contrib
documentation: Additional goodies

classes.name: pgtrigger.contrib.Protect
documentation: A trigger that raises an exception.

classes.name: pgtrigger.contrib.ReadOnly
documentation:
A trigger that prevents edits to fields.

If `fields` are provided, will protect edits to only those fields.
If `exclude` is provided, will protect all fields except the ones
excluded.
If none of these arguments are provided, all fields cannot be edited.

classes.name: pgtrigger.contrib.FSM
documentation:
Enforces a finite state machine on a field.

Supply the trigger with the `field` that transitions and then
a list of tuples of valid transitions to the `transitions` argument.

!!! note

    Only non-null `CharField` fields without quotes are currently supported.
    If your strings have a colon symbol in them, you must override the
    "separator" argument to be a value other than a colon.

classes.name: pgtrigger.contrib.SoftDelete
documentation:
Sets a field to a value when a delete happens.

Supply the trigger with the "field" that will be set
upon deletion and the "value" to which it should be set.
The "value" defaults to `False`.

!!! note

    This trigger currently only supports nullable `BooleanField`,
    `CharField`, and `IntField` fields.

classes.name: pgtrigger.contrib.UpdateSearchVector
documentation:
Updates a `django.contrib.postgres.search.SearchVectorField` from document fields.

Supply the trigger with the `vector_field` that will be updated with
changes to the `document_fields`. Optionally provide a `config_name`, which
defaults to `pg_catalog.english`.

This trigger uses `tsvector_update_trigger` to update the vector field.
See [the Postgres docs](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/textsearch-features.html#TEXTSEARCH-UPDATE-TRIGGERS)
for more information.

!!! note

    `UpdateSearchVector` triggers are not compatible with [pgtrigger.ignore][] since
    it references a built-in trigger. Trying to ignore this trigger results in a
    `RuntimeError`.

classes.name: pgtrigger.compiler.UpsertTriggerSql

methods.name: pgtrigger.compiler.UpsertTriggerSql.get_template
documentation:
This is v1 of the installation template. Do NOT edit
this template unless you are absolutely sure it is
backwards compatible, otherwise it may affect migrations
that reference it.

If it does need to be changed, we will need to introduce
a version variable to be backwards compatible.

Note: Postgres 14 has CREATE OR REPLACE syntax that
we might consider using. This SQL is executed in
a transaction, so dropping and recreating shouldn't
be a problem.

methods.name: pgtrigger.compiler.UpsertTriggerSql.get_defaults
documentation:
These are the default values for the installation
template. Do NOT edit these default values. Keys
may be added, but existing keys should never be updated,
otherwise existing migrations may no longer be correct.

If it does need to be changed, we will need to introduce
a version variable to be backwards compatible.

methods.name: pgtrigger.compiler.UpsertTriggerSql.__init__
documentation: Initialize the SQL and store it in the `.data` attribute.

methods.name: pgtrigger.compiler.UpsertTriggerSql.deconstruct
documentation: Serialize the construction of this class so that it can be used in migrations.

SQL for inserting or updating a trigger

This class is intended to be versionable since migrations
reference it. Older migrations need to be able to point
to earlier versions of the installation template used
for triggers.

methods.name: pgtrigger.compiler._TriggerDdlSql.__init__
documentation: Initialize the SQL and store it in the `.data` attribute.

classes.name: pgtrigger.compiler.DropTriggerSql
documentation:
SQL for dropping a trigger

Triggers are dropped in migrations, so any edits to
the drop trigger template should be backwards compatible
or versioned. I.e. older migrations need to always point to
the SQL here

classes.name: pgtrigger.compiler.EnableTriggerSql
documentation:
SQL for enabling a trigger

We don't currently perform enabling/disabling in migrations,
so this SQL can be changed without consequences to past
migrations.

classes.name: pgtrigger.compiler.DisableTriggerSql
documentation:
SQL for disabling a trigger

We don't currently perform enabling/disabling in migrations,
so this SQL can be changed without consequences to past
migrations.

classes.name: pgtrigger.compiler.Trigger

methods.name: pgtrigger.compiler.Trigger.deconstruct
documentation: Serialize the construction of this class so that it can be used in migrations.

A compiled trigger that's added to internal model state of migrations. It consists
of a name and the trigger SQL for migrations.

modules.name: pgtrigger.models
documentation:
pgtrigger needs an empty models.py in order to get post_migrate signals
needed in its app config

functions.name: pgtrigger.apps.patch_migrations
documentation: Patch the autodetector and model state detection if migrations are turned on

functions.name: pgtrigger.apps.patch_schema_editor
documentation:
Patch the schema editor to allow for column types to be altered on
trigger conditions

functions.name: pgtrigger.apps.register_triggers_from_meta
documentation: Populate the trigger registry from model `Meta.triggers`

methods.name: pgtrigger.apps.PGTriggerConfig.ready
documentation: Do all necessary patching, trigger setup, and signal handler configuration

modules.name: pgtrigger.runtime
documentation:
Functions for runtime-configuration of triggers, such as ignoring
them or dynamically setting the search path.

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime._is_concurrent_statement
documentation: True if the sql statement is concurrent and cannot be ran in a transaction

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime._is_transaction_errored
documentation: True if the current transaction is in an errored state

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime._can_inject_variable
documentation:
True if we can inject a SQL variable into a statement.

A named cursor automatically prepends
"NO SCROLL CURSOR WITHOUT HOLD FOR" to the query, which
causes invalid SQL to be generated. There is no way
to override this behavior in psycopg, so ignoring triggers
cannot happen for named cursors. Django only names cursors
for iterators and other statements that read the database,
so it seems to be safe to ignore named cursors.

Concurrent index creation is also incompatible with local variable
setting. Ignore these cases for now.

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime._inject_pgtrigger_ignore
documentation:
A connection execution wrapper that sets a pgtrigger.ignore
variable in the executed SQL. This lets other triggers know when
they should ignore execution

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime._set_ignore_session_state
documentation: Starts a session where triggers can be ignored

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime._ignore_session
documentation: Starts a session where triggers can be ignored

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime._set_ignore_state
documentation: Manage state to ignore a single URI

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime.ignore
documentation:
Dynamically ignore registered triggers matching URIs from executing in
an individual thread.
If no URIs are provided, ignore all pgtriggers from executing in an
individual thread.

Args:
    *uris: Trigger URIs to ignore. If none are provided, all
        triggers will be ignored.
    databases: The databases to use. If none, all postgres databases
        will be used.

Example:
    Ingore triggers in a context manager:

        with pgtrigger.ignore("my_app.Model:trigger_name"):
            # Do stuff while ignoring trigger

Example:
    Ignore multiple triggers as a decorator:

        @pgtrigger.ignore("my_app.Model:trigger_name", "my_app.Model:other_trigger")
        def my_func():
            # Do stuff while ignoring trigger

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime._inject_schema
documentation:
A connection execution wrapper that sets the schema
variable in the executed SQL.

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime._schema_session
documentation: Starts a session where the search path can be modified

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime.schema
documentation:
Sets the search path to the provided schemas.

If nested, appends the schemas to the search path if not already in it.

Args:
    *schemas: Schemas that should be appended to the search path.
        Schemas already in the search path from nested calls will not be
        appended.
    databases: The databases to set the search path. If none, all postgres
        databases will be used.

functions.name: pgtrigger.runtime.constraints
documentation:
Set deferrable constraint timing for the given triggers, which
will persist until overridden or until end of transaction.
Must be in a transaction to run this.

Args:
    timing: The timing value that overrides the default trigger timing.
    *uris: Trigger URIs over which to set constraint timing.
        If none are provided, all trigger constraint timing will
        be set. All triggers must be deferrable.
    databases: The databases on which to set constraints. If none, all
        postgres databases will be used.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If the database of any triggers is not in a transaction.
    ValueError: If any triggers are not deferrable.

functions.name: pgtrigger.features.model_meta
documentation: True if model meta support is enabled

functions.name: pgtrigger.features.schema_editor
documentation:
True if we are using the patched Postgres schema editor.

Note that setting this to False means that we cannot easily
alter columns of models that are associated with trigger
conditions

functions.name: pgtrigger.features.migrations
documentation: True if migrations are enabled

functions.name: pgtrigger.features.install_on_migrate
documentation: True if triggers should be installed after migrations

functions.name: pgtrigger.features.schema
documentation: The default schema where special objects are installed

functions.name: pgtrigger.features.prune_on_install
documentation: True if triggers should be pruned on a full install or uninstall

functions.name: pgtrigger.migrations._add_trigger
documentation: Add a trigger to a model.

functions.name: pgtrigger.migrations._remove_trigger
documentation: Remove a trigger from a model.

functions.name: pgtrigger.migrations._inject_m2m_dependency_in_proxy
documentation:
Django does not properly add dependencies to m2m fields that are base classes for
proxy models. Inject the dependency here

variables.name: pgtrigger.migrations.OperationDependency
documentation: noqa: E731

methods.name: pgtrigger.migrations.TriggerOperationMixin.allow_migrate_model_trigger
documentation: The check for determinig if a trigger is migrated

classes.name: pgtrigger.migrations.MigrationAutodetectorMixin

methods.name: pgtrigger.migrations.MigrationAutodetectorMixin.create_altered_constraints
documentation:
Piggyback off of constraint generation hooks to generate
trigger migration operations
A mixin that can be subclassed with MigrationAutodetector and detects triggers

classes.name: pgtrigger.migrations.DatabaseSchemaEditorMixin

methods.name: pgtrigger.migrations.DatabaseSchemaEditorMixin.alter_field_type
documentation: Temporarily sets state so that execute() knows we are trying to alter a column type

methods.name: pgtrigger.migrations.DatabaseSchemaEditorMixin._alter_field
documentation: Detects that a field type is being altered and sets the appropriate state

methods.name: pgtrigger.migrations.DatabaseSchemaEditorMixin.temporarily_drop_trigger
documentation:
Given a table and trigger, temporarily drop the trigger and recreate it
after the context manager yields.

methods.name: pgtrigger.migrations.DatabaseSchemaEditorMixin.execute
documentation:
If we are altering a field type, catch the special error psycopg raises
when a column on a trigger is altered. Temporarily drop and recreate
triggers to ensure the alter operation is successful.

methods.name: pgtrigger.migrations.DatabaseSchemaEditorMixin.create_model
documentation:
Create the model

`Meta.triggers` isn't populated on the forwards `CreateTable` migration
(as Triggers are only added to the migration state via `AddTrigger`
operations). `Meta.triggers` may be populated when:

- The backwards operation of a `RemoveTable` operation where there was
  still triggers defined in the model state when the table was
  removed.
- Creating the tables of an unmigrated app when `run_syncdb` is
  supplied to the migrate command (or when running tests).

A schema editor mixin that can subclass a DatabaseSchemaEditor and
handle altering column types of triggers.

Postgres does not allow altering column types of columns used in trigger
conditions. Here we fix this with the following approach:

1. Detect that a column type is being changed and set a flag so that we
   can alter behavior of the schema editor.
2. In execute(), check for the special error message that's raised
   when trying to alter a column of a trigger. Temporarily drop triggers
   during the alter statement and reinstall them. Ensure this is all
   wrapped in a transaction

classes.name: pgtrigger.management.commands.pgtrigger.SubCommands
documentation:
Subcommand class vendored in from
https://github.com/andrewp-as-is/django-subcommands.py
because of installation issues

classes.name: pgtrigger.management.commands.pgtrigger.BaseSchemaCommand
documentation: Sets the search path based on any "schema" option that's found

functions.name: pgtrigger.core._cleanup_on_exit
documentation: Allows Trigger methods to be used as context managers, mostly for
testing purposes

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.Row
documentation: For specifying row-level triggers (the default)

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.Statement
documentation: For specifying statement-level triggers

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.Before
documentation: For specifying `BEFORE` in the when clause of a trigger.

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.After
documentation: For specifying `AFTER` in the when clause of a trigger.

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.InsteadOf
documentation: For specifying `INSTEAD OF` in the when clause of a trigger.

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.Update
documentation: For specifying `UPDATE` as the trigger operation.

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.Delete
documentation: For specifying `DELETE` as the trigger operation.

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.Truncate
documentation: For specifying `TRUNCATE` as the trigger operation.

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.Insert
documentation: For specifying `INSERT` as the trigger operation.

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.Immediate
documentation: For deferrable triggers that run immediately by default

variables.name: pgtrigger.core.Deferred
documentation: For deferrable triggers that run at the end of the transaction by default

classes.name: pgtrigger.core._Primitive
documentation: Boilerplate for some of the primitive operations

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.Referencing
documentation: For specifying the `REFERENCING` clause of a statement-level trigger

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.Operations
documentation:
For providing multiple operations `OR`ed together.

Note that using the `|` operator is preferred syntax.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.UpdateOf
documentation: For specifying `UPDATE OF` as the trigger operation.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.Condition
documentation: For specifying free-form SQL in the condition of a trigger.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core._OldNewQuery
documentation:
A special Query object for referencing the `OLD` and `NEW` variables in a
trigger. Only used by the [pgtrigger.Q][] object.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.F
documentation:
Similar to Django's `F` object, allows referencing the old and new
rows in a trigger condition.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.IsDistinctFrom
documentation:
A custom `IS DISTINCT FROM` field lookup for common trigger conditions.
For example, `pgtrigger.Q(old__field__df=pgtrigger.F("new__field"))`.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.IsNotDistinctFrom
documentation:
A custom `IS NOT DISTINCT FROM` field lookup for common trigger conditions.
For example, `pgtrigger.Q(old__field__ndf=pgtrigger.F("new__field"))`.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.Q
documentation:
Similar to Django's `Q` object, allows building filter clauses based
on the old and new rows in a trigger condition.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core._Change
documentation:
For specifying a condition based on changes to fields.

See child classes for more documentation on arguments.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.AnyChange

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.AnyChange.__init__
documentation:
If any supplied fields change, trigger the condition.

Args:
    *fields: If any supplied fields change, trigger the condition.
        If no fields are supplied, defaults to all fields on the model.
    exclude: Fields to exclude.
    exclude_auto: Exclude all `auto_now` and `auto_now_add` fields automatically.
If any supplied fields change, trigger the condition.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.AnyDontChange

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.AnyDontChange.__init__
documentation:
If any supplied fields don't change, trigger the condition.

Args:
    *fields: If any supplied fields don't change, trigger the condition.
        If no fields are supplied, defaults to all fields on the model.
    exclude: Fields to exclude.
    exclude_auto: Exclude all `auto_now` and `auto_now_add` fields automatically.
If any supplied fields don't change, trigger the condition.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.AllChange

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.AllChange.__init__
documentation:
If all supplied fields change, trigger the condition.

Args:
    *fields: If all supplied fields change, trigger the condition.
        If no fields are supplied, defaults to all fields on the model.
    exclude: Fields to exclude.
    exclude_auto: Exclude all `auto_now` and `auto_now_add` fields automatically.
If all supplied fields change, trigger the condition.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.AllDontChange

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.AllDontChange.__init__
documentation:
If all supplied fields don't change, trigger the condition.

Args:
    *fields: If all supplied fields don't change, trigger the condition.
        If no fields are supplied, defaults to all fields on the model.
    exclude: Fields to exclude.
    exclude_auto: Exclude all `auto_now` and `auto_now_add` fields automatically.
If all supplied don't fields change, trigger the condition.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.Func

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Func.render
documentation:
Render the SQL of the function.

Args:
    model: The model.

Returns:
    The rendered SQL.

Allows for rendering a function with access to the "meta", "fields",
and "columns" variables of the current model.

For example, `func=Func("SELECT {columns.id} FROM {meta.db_table};")` makes it
possible to do inline SQL in the `Meta` of a model and reference its properties.

classes.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.validate_name
documentation:
Verifies the name is under the maximum length and has valid characters.

Raises:
    ValueError: If the name is invalid

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.get_pgid
documentation:
The ID of the trigger and function object in postgres

All objects are prefixed with "pgtrigger_" in order to be
discovered/managed by django-pgtrigger.

Args:
    model: The model.

Returns:
    The Postgres ID.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.get_condition
documentation:
Get the condition of the trigger.

Args:
    model: The model.

Returns:
    The condition.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.get_declare
documentation:
Gets the DECLARE part of the trigger function if any variables
are used.

Args:
    model: The model

Returns:
    A list of variable name / type tuples that will
    be shown in the DECLARE. For example [('row_data', 'JSONB')]

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.get_func
documentation:
Returns the trigger function that comes between the BEGIN and END
clause.

Args:
    model: The model

Returns:
    The trigger function as a SQL string or [pgtrigger.Func][] object.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.get_uri
documentation:
The URI for the trigger.

Args:
    model: The model

Returns:
    The URI in the format of the "<app>.<model>:<trigger>"

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.render_condition
documentation:
Renders the condition SQL in the trigger declaration.

Args:
    model: The model.

Returns:
    The rendered condition SQL

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.render_declare
documentation:
Renders the DECLARE of the trigger function, if any.

Args:
    model: The model.

Returns:
    The rendered declare SQL.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.render_execute
documentation:
Renders what should be executed by the trigger. This defaults
to the trigger function.

Args:
    model: The model

Returns:
    The SQL for the execution of the trigger function.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.render_func
documentation:
Renders the func.

Args:
    model: The model

Returns:
    The rendered SQL of the trigger function

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.compile
documentation:
Create a compiled representation of the trigger. useful for migrations.

Args:
    model: The model

Returns:
    The compiled trigger object.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.allow_migrate
documentation:
True if the trigger for this model can be migrated.

Defaults to using the router's allow_migrate.

Args:
    model: The model.
    database: The name of the database configuration.

Returns:
    `True` if the trigger for the model can be migrated.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.format_sql
documentation:
Returns SQL as one line that has trailing whitespace removed from each line.

Args:
    sql: The unformatted SQL

Returns:
    The formatted SQL

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.exec_sql
documentation:
Conditionally execute SQL if migrations are allowed.

Args:
    sql: The SQL string.
    model: The model.
    database: The name of the database configuration.
    fetchall: True if all results should be fetched

Returns:
    A psycopg cursor result

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.get_installation_status
documentation:
Returns the installation status of a trigger.

The return type is (status, enabled), where status is one of:

1. `INSTALLED`: If the trigger is installed
2. `UNINSTALLED`: If the trigger is not installed
3. `OUTDATED`: If the trigger is installed but has been modified
4. `IGNORED`: If migrations are not allowed

"enabled" is True if the trigger is installed and enabled or false
if installed and disabled (or uninstalled).

Args:
    model: The model.
    database: The name of the database configuration.

Returns:
    A tuple with the installation and enablement status.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.register
documentation:
Register model classes with the trigger

Args:
    *models: Models to register to this trigger.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.unregister
documentation:
Unregister model classes with the trigger.

Args:
    *models: Models to unregister to this trigger.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.install
documentation:
Installs the trigger for a model.

Args:
    model: The model.
    database: The name of the database configuration.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.uninstall
documentation:
Uninstalls the trigger for a model.

Args:
    model: The model.
    database: The name of the database configuration.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.enable
documentation:
Enables the trigger for a model.

Args:
    model: The model.
    database: The name of the database configuration.

methods.name: pgtrigger.core.Trigger.disable
documentation:
Disables the trigger for a model.

Args:
    model: The model.
    database: The name of the database configuration.

For specifying a free-form PL/pgSQL trigger function or for
creating derived trigger classes.
